I scanned the top 48 Next.js projects on GitHub for 2026 best-practice gaps.

Average score: 68/100

92% still use manual useMemo/useCallback (React Compiler made it obsolete)
90% use inline styles instead of Tailwind
81% have console.log in production

Try it: npx axiom-check

92% of the top Next.js repos on GitHub still use manual useMemo/useCallback.

React Compiler handles memoization automatically in 2026.

You're writing code the machine would rather write for you.

npx axiom-check

38% of the top Next.js repos that touch Supabase don't have Row Level Security.

That's a tenant data leak waiting to happen.

Scan yours: npx axiom-check

Hall of Fame — cleanest Next.js repos I scanned:

1. srbhr/Resume-Matcher — 86/100
2. SigNoz/signoz — 85/100
3. vercel/swr — 85/100
4. appwrite/appwrite — 81/100
5. dyad-sh/dyad — 80/100

What's your score? npx axiom-check

69% of top Next.js repos are still shipping Tailwind v3 deprecated class names.

bg-gradient-to-* → bg-linear-to-*
flex-shrink-0 → shrink-0

They'll break the day you upgrade to Tailwind v4.

Check yours: npx axiom-check

Axiom's Knowledge Accelerator does web research daily across 11 domains.

Findings go into a spaced repetition engine (day 1, 3, 7, 14).

By day 14 the pattern is either in your long-term memory or archived.

That's how you actually learn from research, not just bookmark it.

Every AI agent framework runs tasks. None of them learn from the outcomes.

Axiom's Auto-Skill Extraction captures successful build patterns and turns them into reusable skills automatically.

Your AI literally evolves from its own experience.

github.com/yorkisestevez/axiom

Axiom's Cross-Project Sync detects when a pattern in one project is missing from another.

You built RLS in Project A. Project B doesn't have it yet. The system flags it.

No more 'wait, didn't I solve this already?'

Axiom is hardware-aware. It knows your GPU's VRAM budget, your loaded Ollama models, and your process registry.

It won't try to load an 18GB model when you have 12GB of VRAM.

No other agent framework does this.

Repeated corrections in Axiom auto-promote to permanent rules via a staging system.

Correct it twice, the system proposes a new rule for your review. Approve or reject.

Your AI learns from its mistakes, then stops making them.

Axiom's wiki is git-versioned and Obsidian-compatible.

AI models NEVER directly edit wiki files. All updates go through a staging system.

Your knowledge is protected from model hallucination, while still benefiting from automation.

github.com/yorkisestevez/axiom

Tip: Enable FLASH_ATTENTION=1 in your Ollama startup.

Reduces KV cache by 5-10%. On a 12GB VRAM GPU, that's the difference between fitting a 7B model at 32K context or spilling to CPU.

5-20x speedup for one env var.

Multi-tenant SaaS tip: Add RLS policies BEFORE you ship.

Retrofitting tenant isolation later is 'rebuilding the foundation while people live in the house.'

Every tenant table needs RLS + a composite index on (tenant_id, created_at).

npx axiom-check flags this.

Stripe deprecated the legacy usage records API in 2025-03-31.

If you're still using create_usage_record, migrate to the Meters API before your integration breaks.

axiom-check detects this: npx axiom-check

Stop writing useMemo and useCallback by hand.

React Compiler handles memoization automatically. Your manual calls are either redundant or actively worse than what the compiler would do.

2026 React codebases should have zero manual memoization.

Tip for Next.js App Router: Default to Server Components.

Only add 'use client' when you need useState, useEffect, event handlers, or browser APIs.

Most of your 'use client' directives are unnecessary and shipping extra JS to users.

Every API route handler needs input validation at the boundary.

Use Zod. Use safeParse, not parse. Return structured validation errors.

Never trust the request body. Ever.

axiom-check catches missing validation: npx axiom-check

Error boundaries aren't optional in production React apps.

One uncaught error in billing shouldn't take down the whole app.

Add error.tsx to every route segment in Next.js App Router. Takes 5 minutes per route.

Scan your repo for 2026 best-practice gaps right now. Zero install:

npx axiom-check

Takes 5 seconds. Returns a score out of 100 and 12 specific gaps.

Free. MIT licensed.

github.com/yorkisestevez/axiom

Curious how your codebase scores?

npx axiom-check

Share your score in the replies. I'll DM the folks with 90+ scores — I want to know what you're doing right.

Axiom is open source and MIT licensed.

If you've wanted an AI operating system that actually learns from every session, this is it.

14 core modules. Zero external dependencies. Works with Claude Code, Ollama, any LLM.

github.com/yorkisestevez/axiom

The Axiom framework is free and open source.

I monetize via domain packs — pre-built configs for specific verticals.

SaaS Starter Pack: 5 skills, 3 research domains, 6 gap rules.

$49 on Gumroad. Ships with Axiom.

yorkistevez.gumroad.com/l/axiom-saas-starter

The problem with every AI agent framework in 2026:

They run tasks. They don't learn from them.

You discover a pattern Monday → forget it by Wednesday. Same bugs across every project. Research evaporates into bookmarks.

Fine for demos. Useless for daily work.

Memory without retrieval is a journal.
Memory without spaced repetition is a cache that expires.
Memory without reinforcement is a hallucination waiting to happen.

Axiom's learning stack has all three. That's why it actually gets smarter over time.

The unsexy truth about 'AI that learns':

Most of it is just logging. Real learning requires:
1. Capture
2. Spaced review
3. Pattern detection
4. Promotion to rules
5. Feedback on outcomes

If your framework skips any step, it's not learning — it's remembering.

Hot take: Most 'AI operating systems' on GitHub aren't operating systems.

An OS needs:
- Process management (VRAM, RAM, CPU budgeting)
- Knowledge persistence (not just context windows)
- Self-modification with guardrails
- Multi-agent coordination

Axiom has all four.

The best AI tool isn't the one with the biggest model.

It's the one that remembers what worked yesterday, learned from today's mistakes, and is researching tomorrow's patterns while you sleep.

That's Axiom.

Built Axiom because I run 15 projects and my AI kept forgetting what worked.

Patterns from Monday were gone by Wednesday. Research I did disappeared into bookmarks. Same bugs across different projects.

So I built a framework that actually remembers. Now it's open source.

Axiom is 14 core modules, zero external dependencies, 5,500 lines of Node.js.

Every line is there because I needed it for my own projects. Nothing built on speculation.

Open source because if it helps me, it might help you.

github.com/yorkisestevez/axiom

The axiom-check scanner was a Trojan horse.

People use it because it's free and useful. Then they realize it's from a bigger framework that does 10x more.

Started with 12 rules. Ran it against the top 48 Next.js repos. Found patterns I didn't expect.

Every week, Axiom runs a Hall of Gaps scan against a different topic on GitHub.

Next.js, Supabase, React, Claude Code, Ollama, TypeScript, Tailwind.

Results publish to the Axiom blog automatically. You get fresh data on what's actually happening in real codebases every Sunday.

axiom-check rule #1 — Missing Row Level Security.

Severity: HIGH

Found in 38% of top Next.js repos that use Supabase.

This is a tenant data leak waiting to happen. Add RLS policies before you ship.

Details: github.com/yorkisestevez/axiom-check

axiom-check rule #2 — Hardcoded secrets.

Severity: HIGH

Found in 25% of top Next.js repos.

SK_LIVE_, PK_LIVE_, ghp_, AKIA... all detected with regex.

Move them to .env. Add .env to .gitignore. Rotate the keys.

axiom-check rule #4 — Tailwind v3 deprecated classes.

Severity: MEDIUM

Found in 69% of top Next.js repos.

bg-gradient-to-* → bg-linear-to-*
flex-shrink-0 → shrink-0

Will break on v4 migration.

axiom-check rule #6 — TypeScript strict mode disabled.

Severity: MEDIUM

Found in 15% of top Next.js repos.

Strict mode in tsconfig catches half your runtime bugs at compile time. Turn it on.

axiom-check rule #9 — Manual useMemo/useCallback.

Severity: LOW

Found in 92% of top Next.js repos.

React Compiler handles this automatically. Your manual memoization is either redundant or actively worse than the compiler.

Spaced repetition is proven science. Anki. Duolingo. SuperMemo.

Nobody applies it to AI learning.

Axiom does: findings from daily research resurface at day 1, 3, 7, 14. By day 14 it's either in your long-term memory or archived.

The Feynman technique for AI:

1. Your AI captures a successful build pattern
2. Names it plainly
3. Links it to the project + files it worked in
4. Tests it against similar problems
5. Promotes it to a reusable skill if it holds up

That's auto-skill extraction. That's Axiom.

Learning requires feedback.

The correction engine in Axiom tracks every 'no, that's wrong' you give it. After the same correction 2+ times, it auto-proposes a permanent rule for your config file.

You stop having to correct the same mistake. Ever.

Axiom is MIT licensed, zero external dependencies, runs on any Node 18+.

If you have 10 minutes, clone it and run the setup wizard. If you have 1 minute, run npx axiom-check.

Feedback, stars, PRs welcome.

github.com/yorkisestevez/axiom

Open a PR with a new gap rule and I'll ship it.

Every week axiom-check gets better because someone in the community caught a pattern I missed.

Your weird codebase is exactly what makes it stronger.

github.com/yorkisestevez/axiom-check

Ran axiom-check against a repo and got a low score?

The score isn't a grade. It's a backlog.

Each gap has a specific fix action you can knock out in an hour. Score climbs fast once you start.

github.com/yorkisestevez/axiom

Current: Gumroad only. Follow for updates.

Quick poll — what matters most in your AI tool?

A) Speed
B) Memory between sessions
C) Code quality detection
D) Cross-project sync

Reply with a letter. I'll build the thing most people pick into Axiom v2.

What's the worst AI 'learning' you've ever seen?

Me: A tool that stored every chat in a 10MB text file and called it 'memory.'

Replies with the best examples.

(This is why I built Axiom.)
